summary: DHH applauds Google's decision to immediately fire 28 employees who occupied the Google Cloud CEO's office to protest the company's contract with Israel. He frames this as a vindication of the position he and Basecamp took three years earlier: that workplaces should not be platforms for political activism. He argues that Google's follow-up policy banning political activism at work signals that corporate America has passed the peak of ideological capture. DHH connects this to a broader pattern including Coinbase's similar stance and sees it as evidence that common-sense workplace norms are being restored.
key_points: Google fired 28 employees immediately for occupying an office in political protest, with no drawn-out investigation or sympathetic statements
Google followed up by explicitly banning political activism in the workplace, echoing policies from Coinbase and Basecamp
DHH sees this as proof that corporate America has moved past peak 'ideological capture' that he says nearly swallowed companies whole
The specific political cause is irrelevant — DHH argues the principle applies equally to any activist cause used to disrupt business
Three years ago this stance drew massive backlash; now DHH predicts it will barely cause a ripple outside activist echo chambers
money_quotes: Anyone watching that shit show would be right to wonder whether one of America's great technology companies had fallen completely into the hands of the new theocracy.

But then the most amazing thing happened. There was no drawn-out investigation. No saccharine statements about employee's rights to occupy offices, preventing work from happening, or advance their political agenda at work. Nope. They were just fired. Immediately. All 28 of them.

Three years ago, taking a common-sense position like this would have been met with drama and outage in the media and on Twitter. Today I doubt it'll bring more than a ripple outside of a few activist echo chambers on Mastodon. Amazing progress!
We've not just passed the peak of the nonsense that nearly swallowed corporate America whole, but we're now seeing them repudiate it head on.
If Google, with it's employment roster still packed with people sympathetic to the new theocracy, can put its foot down, so can the rest of the Fortune 500 and beyond.
key_insight: Google's swift firing of activist employees and adoption of a no-politics-at-work policy validates what DHH and others advocated years earlier: workplaces are for business, not political platforms.
